required.title: [For Mentor] The Whole-Brain Child
required.author: Daniel J. Siegel and Tina Payne Bryson

required.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Stories of people who did hard things

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] The Little House
suggested.author: series by Laura Ingalls Wilder

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] No-Drama Discipline
supplementary.author: Daniel J. Siegel and Tina Payne Bryson

supplementary.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Biographies appropriate to age

competence_markers.description: They follow their daily routine without adult prompting on most days (not all — most; consistency is the trend, not perfection)
They complete tasks fully — cleanup included — before moving to the next activity
They respond to reasonable instructions the first time, without habitual negotiation or delay
They can wait for something they want without repeated asking, emotional escalation, or prolonged distress
They stop an activity when told to, promptly and without extended resistance
They can articulate the difference between a want and a need, with examples
They take ownership of their physical space — room, workspace, shared areas — leaving them in acceptable condition
They engage in the weekly review honestly, identifying both successes and struggles

narration_prompts.detail: Walk through it step by step. What part do you do best? What part do you sometimes forget?
What does it look like when it is done right? How do you feel when you finish it?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you had to wait for something you really wanted.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you do while you waited? Was it hard?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between something you want and something you need?
Tell me about someone from a story who did something hard because it was the right thing to do.
narration_prompts.detail: What did they do? Why was it hard? Would you have done the same thing?

narration_prompts.prompt: What was the hardest part of this week?
narration_prompts.detail: Not schoolwork — the hardest part of doing what you were supposed to do.

cross_connections.description: The weekly review and narration prompts are the earliest form of self-examination. The child who learns to reflect on "what was hard this week?" at age 6 is building the capacity for genuine self-examination at age 16.
The execution partner to IC1.1. Where IC1.1 forms the character, IC2.1 builds the daily systems. IC2.1-A launches in Phase 2 immediately after IC1.1-A1 competence markers are met.

Waiting, stopping, and tolerating discomfort are the earliest resilience training. IC3.1-A formalizes the emotional awareness that IC1.1-A1 introduces through daily life. Both run concurrently in Phase 2.
The literacy track launches formally in Phase 2 alongside IC1.1-A1's continuing practices. The habit structure built here enables the daily writing practice that 3.2.1-A requires.
The numeracy track also launches in Phase 2. The ability to follow routines, complete tasks, and sustain attention — all IC1.1-A1 outputs — are what make daily math practice possible.
The physical track begins formally in Phase 2. The self-control and routine compliance developed here translate directly into the discipline required for daily physical training.
The first knowledge domain content begins in Phase 2 through read-alouds and oral narration. The listening skills and attention span built through IC1.1-A1 routines make this engagement possible.

content.why_this_matters:
This packet is different from every other packet in the framework. It does not teach you a skill you practice on weekends. It shapes how you live every day — how you wake up, how you take care of your responsibilities, how you handle the moments when you want something but cannot have it yet, and how you finish what you start.

You might wonder why this matters. After all, anyone can brush their teeth or make their bed. But here is the truth that most adults never learn: **the person who cannot govern their own daily life cannot govern anything else.** The person who cannot finish a small task will not finish a large one. The person who falls apart when they have to wait will not survive difficulty. The person who only does what they feel like doing will never build anything that requires effort past the point where it stops being fun.

Everything in this framework — the fire-building, the reading, the physical training, the leadership — depends on what happens inside you before any of it begins. That is what this packet builds.

You will not notice it working. It does not feel like learning a new thing. It feels like Tuesday. It feels like making your bed again. It feels like waiting when you do not want to wait. But over months and years, these small repeated actions become who you are — not what you do, but who you are. And that person can do things that the person without these habits cannot.
content.what_this_develops:
This packet does not have a checklist of things "you will be able to do" the way a skills packet does. Instead, it develops **patterns of behavior** that are observed over time:

1. **Following a daily routine** without being reminded more than once
2. **Completing tasks fully** before moving to the next thing — including cleanup
3. **Responding to reasonable authority** the first time, without negotiation or delay
4. **Waiting** — tolerating the gap between wanting something and getting it, without falling apart
5. **Stopping** when asked — the first and most fundamental form of self-control
6. **Distinguishing want from need** — beginning to understand that not every desire requires fulfillment
7. **Owning your space** — leaving things better than or equal to how you found them
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Daily Routine — Structure Before Freedom

A daily routine is not a punishment. It is a scaffold. The same way a plant needs a stake to grow straight before it is strong enough to stand alone, a child needs external structure before they can generate internal structure.

**The routine is:**
- Wake at a consistent time
- Hygiene (face, teeth, dress) without being told each step
- Make your bed — every morning, without exception
- Eat breakfast
- Complete morning responsibilities (chores, feeding animals, tidying)
- Begin the day's work (schoolwork, learning, practice)
- Midday responsibilities
- Afternoon work or activity
- Evening responsibilities (cleanup, preparation for tomorrow)
- Bedtime routine — consistent, predictable, non-negotiable

**The point is not the specific tasks.** The point is that the child inhabits a rhythm. Rhythm becomes predictability. Predictability becomes security. Security becomes the platform from which self-governance eventually grows.

**For the youngest students (4-5):** The routine is externalized. A visual schedule on the wall — pictures or simple words showing what comes next. The child follows the schedule, checks things off, and builds the habit of sequential completion. The adult supervises but does not narrate each step unless the child is stuck.

**For older Stretching Branches students (6-9):** The routine becomes internalized. The visual schedule may still exist, but the child should be able to move through their morning without being told what comes next. The test is: can you get from waking to ready-for-the-day without an adult prompting each step?

### Part 2: Completing Tasks Fully

Half-done is not done. This is a principle the child learns physically, through small tasks, long before they understand it conceptually.

- If you take out the blocks, you put them back. All of them. In the container. On the shelf.
- If you start making your bed, you finish making your bed. Not "mostly done." Done.
- If you are asked to set the table, the table is set — plates, utensils, napkins, cups. Not plates only because you got distracted.

**The standard is "complete."** Not perfect — complete. A bed made roughly by a five-year-old who pulled the covers up and straightened the pillow has met the standard. A bed left half-made by a nine-year-old who wandered off to do something more interesting has not.

This principle scales directly into every other domain. The student who learns to complete small tasks fully at age 5 will complete research projects, physical training sessions, and multi-day field exercises at age 15. The student who learns that "good enough" is acceptable will carry that habit forever.

### Part 3: Obedience to Reasonable Authority

This is the most counter-cultural section of this packet. Modern culture is deeply suspicious of obedience — and for understandable reasons, since history is full of examples where obedience was demanded in service of cruelty. But the answer to bad authority is not the elimination of authority. It is the cultivation of the ability to obey reasonable authority — which is the prerequisite for eventually exercising authority well.

**What this looks like:**
- When a parent says "come here," the child comes. Not after finishing what they are doing. Not after negotiating. They come.
- When a parent says "stop," the child stops. Immediately. This is a safety requirement first, and a self-governance requirement second.
- When a parent gives an instruction, the child follows it the first time. Repeated instructions for the same task indicate a formation gap, not a hearing problem.

**What this does NOT look like:**
- Blind obedience to any adult for any reason
- Suppression of questions — the child may ask "why?" after they comply, not instead of complying
- Obedience driven by fear rather than trust and respect

**The sequence matters:** First, the child learns to respond to authority. Then, as they develop reasoning, they learn WHY the authority structures exist. Then, much later, they learn to exercise authority themselves. The child who never learns to follow cannot lead. The child who only follows and never questions becomes something different — compliant, not governed. IC1.1 builds the first step: the will that can follow before it can lead.

### Part 4: Learning to Wait

Impulse control develops over years. The young child is not expected to have an adult's patience. But the capacity to wait — to tolerate the gap between desire and fulfillment — is trained, not inborn.

**Training ground:**
- "Dinner is in twenty minutes." The child waits. They do not demand a snack. They occupy themselves.
- "You can have that after you finish your work." The reward follows the effort. Not before, not alongside.
- "We will go to the park after lunch." The plan does not change because the child asks ten times.
- "I will answer your question when I am done talking." The child waits their turn.

The key: **consistency.** If "after dinner" sometimes means "after dinner" and sometimes means "fine, here, just stop asking," the lesson is: persistence works. The child learns to escalate, not to wait.

Every time the stated boundary holds, the child's capacity to tolerate delay grows slightly. Over hundreds of repetitions, this becomes a person who can delay gratification — which is, by a large body of research, one of the strongest predictors of life success across every measurable domain.

### Part 5: Learning to Stop

"Stop" is the first and most fundamental self-control command. Before a child can start difficult things, finish long things, or choose hard things, they must be able to stop a thing they are doing when told to.

- Stop running. Stop talking. Stop throwing. Stop hitting.
- The child who cannot stop when an external signal says "stop" cannot yet stop when an internal signal says "stop." The external practice precedes the internal capacity.

This is not about crushing the child's spirit. It is about building the neural circuitry of inhibition — the ability to interrupt an ongoing action. This circuitry is literally underdeveloped in young children (prefrontal cortex maturation continues into the mid-twenties). Training provides the experience that accelerates what biology will eventually complete.

**Practice:** Games that involve stopping — freeze dance, red light/green light, Simon Says. These are not just games. They are self-regulation training in a format the child enjoys.

### Part 6: Want vs. Need

The child begins to learn one of the most important cognitive distinctions in human development: the difference between something they want and something they need.

- "I'm hungry" (need) vs. "I want ice cream" (want)
- "I need shoes" (need) vs. "I want those shoes" (want)
- "I need to use the bathroom" (need) vs. "I don't want to go to bed" (want)

This is not about denying wants. It is about building the cognitive framework that allows the child to categorize their desires accurately. A child who understands the difference between want and need can:
- Accept that not all wants will be fulfilled
- Prioritize needs over wants without drama
- Eventually — in Weathering Storms and C — make resource allocation decisions, budget, and sacrifice short-term wants for long-term needs

The foundation is laid here, in small conversations: "I hear that you want that. Is it something you need, or something you want?" The child begins to sort. The sorting itself is the skill.

### Part 7: Owning Your Space

Responsibility starts with physical space. The child is responsible for the state they leave things in.

- If you used the table, wipe it.
- If you played in the yard, bring in what you brought out.
- If you were in a room, it should not look worse after you leave than before you arrived.

This is not cleaning as punishment. It is the earliest form of stewardship — the understanding that the spaces you use are shared, and that leaving them degraded is a failure of responsibility. The child who does this automatically at age 7 becomes the team member who cleans up the campsite, the employee who leaves the workspace ready for the next person, the leader who does not leave messes for others to handle.
content.formative_practices:
These are not one-time activities. They are ongoing daily and weekly practices that form the habits IC1.1 develops.

### Practice 1: The Morning Routine [Daily]
Follow your morning routine from wake to ready-for-the-day. For younger students, use a visual schedule. For older students, do it from memory. The measure: can you complete the full routine without an adult prompting each step? Track this weekly — how many days did you need no reminders?

### Practice 2: Chore Ownership [Daily]
You have at least one chore that is entirely yours. Not "helping" — owning. You are responsible for it being done, done fully, and done on time. It does not matter if you feel like doing it. The chore does not care about your feelings. It needs to be done.

### Practice 3: Cleanup Protocol [After Every Activity]
When you finish an activity — building, drawing, playing, eating — you clean up before you begin the next thing. Not later. Not when reminded. Before you move on. This is practiced until it is automatic.

### Practice 4: Waiting Practice [As Opportunities Arise]
When you want something and are told to wait, you wait. Without asking again. Without counting down. Without drama. You find something to do in the meantime. Each successful wait builds the capacity for the next one.

### Practice 5: Weekly Review [Weekly, With Mentor]
At the end of each week, sit with your mentor for 5-10 minutes. Review: What went well this week? Where did you need reminders? What was hardest? What are you going to work on next week? This is not punishment. It is reflection — the earliest form of self-examination that IC1.6 will develop further.

### Practice 6: Stop Games [2-3 Times Per Week]
Play games that require stopping on command: freeze dance, red light/green light, Simon Says, or variations your family invents. These are self-regulation training. The child who becomes excellent at freeze dance is building the same neural circuitry they will use to stop themselves from saying the wrong thing at age 14.

### Practice 7: Read-Aloud Stories of Character [Weekly]
At least once a week, read aloud a story — biography, folk tale, historical account — of someone who demonstrated discipline, persistence, or self-control. After the story, discuss: What did they do? Was it hard? Why did they do it? What would have happened if they hadn't?

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Your Consistency Is the Curriculum

Everything in this packet depends on one variable: **whether the adult maintains the standard consistently.** If bedtime is 8:00 PM, it is 8:00 PM — not 8:00 PM on good days and 9:15 PM when you are tired. If the rule is "clean up before you start the next thing," that rule holds on Saturday morning and on the exhausting Wednesday afternoon when enforcing it takes more energy than you have.

This is hard. It is harder than teaching fire-building or reviewing history. It requires sustained parental discipline — which is IC1 applied to yourself before you apply it to the child. The child will internalize whatever standard you actually maintain, not the standard you state.

### Emotional Consistency

Correction must be calm and proportionate. The same misbehavior should produce the same response regardless of your mood. The child who receives dramatic anger for a minor infraction on Tuesday and indifferent tolerance for the same infraction on Thursday learns nothing about the infraction — they learn that the adult is unpredictable. Unpredictability produces anxiety, not self-governance.

**The correction formula:**
1. Name the behavior: "You left your blocks out."
2. State the expectation: "We clean up before we start something new."
3. Direct the correction: "Go put the blocks away."
4. Follow through: verify it is done.
5. No extended lecture, no emotional escalation, no shaming. It is done. Move on.

### What You Model

The child will become what you do, not what you say. If you want the child to make their bed, make yours. If you want the child to follow through on commitments, follow through on yours — including the ones you made to the child. If you want the child to handle frustration without meltdown, they need to see you handle frustration without meltdown.

This does not mean performing perfection. It means living with visible integrity: "I forgot to do that, and I should have. I'm going to do it now." The child who sees an adult own a mistake and correct it is learning IC1.3 (integrity) and IC1.4 (failure recovery) at the same time as IC1.1.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Daily Routine Without Prompting

**Developing well:** The child moves through their morning routine (wake, hygiene, bed, dress, breakfast) without step-by-step reminders on most mornings. They may use a visual schedule. They may occasionally need a single prompt ("Have you made your bed?") but not a sequence of instructions.

**Developing slowly:** The child requires prompting for every step, every morning, without improvement over weeks. They begin the routine but stall midway without adult re-engagement.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the routine too complex for the child's age? (Simplify.) Is the visual schedule clear? (Revise.) Is the adult inconsistent about when the routine is enforced? (Fix the adult, not the child.) Is the child getting enough sleep? (A tired child cannot self-regulate.)

### 2. Task Completion

**Developing well:** The child finishes what they start. Toys are put away. Art supplies are returned. Chores are completed to the defined standard. The child may need occasional reminders but the trend is toward independent completion.

**Developing slowly:** The child habitually abandons tasks halfway. Cleanup is incomplete. Chores are done partially and declared "done."

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the task age-appropriate in scope? (A 4-year-old cannot organize an entire room. They can put blocks in one bin.) Is the standard clear? (Does the child know what "done" looks like?) Is there a consistent consequence for incomplete work? (Not punishment — the natural consequence: you don't start the next thing until this thing is finished.)

### 3. Response to Authority

**Developing well:** The child responds to instructions the first time in most cases. They may ask "why?" after complying. They stop when told to stop.

**Developing slowly:** The child habitually ignores the first instruction, requiring escalation. They negotiate before complying. "Stop" produces a few more seconds of the behavior before cessation.

**Critical distinction:** A child who sometimes needs a second prompt is normal. A child who NEVER responds to the first prompt and has learned to wait for escalation has been trained — by the adult — to ignore the first instruction. The fix is in the adult's response: if the first instruction does not produce compliance, the consequence follows the first instruction, not the third.

### 4. Waiting

**Developing well:** The child can wait for a stated interval (10 minutes, "after lunch," "when I'm done") without repeated asking, emotional escalation, or meltdown. They find something to do in the meantime.

**Developing slowly:** The child asks repeatedly, escalates emotionally, or melts down when told to wait. The interval tolerance is not improving over weeks.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you maintaining the stated wait time? (If "after dinner" sometimes becomes "okay fine, here" when the child escalates, you are training escalation.) Is the wait time age-appropriate? (A 4-year-old can wait 5 minutes. A 9-year-old can wait 30.) Are you naming the wait period concretely? ("In 15 minutes" is more manageable than "later" for a young child.)

### 5. Stopping

**Developing well:** The child stops a physical or verbal activity promptly when told to stop. In stop-games (freeze dance, red light/green light), they have fast response times and enjoy the challenge.

**Developing slowly:** "Stop" produces a delayed response. The child continues for several seconds after being told to stop. In physical play, they cannot freeze on command.

**Note:** This is a neurological development issue as much as a behavioral one. A 4-year-old has genuinely less inhibitory control than an 8-year-old. The question is whether the trend is improving, not whether the response is instant.

### 6. Want vs. Need

**Developing well:** The child can categorize their desires when asked. "Is that a want or a need?" produces an accurate answer most of the time. They begin to self-categorize without prompting.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot distinguish between the two, or categorizes everything as a need. "I NEED ice cream" is genuinely believed.

**How to build this:** Conversational practice. Every time the child states a desire, occasionally ask: "Is that a want or a need?" Do not make it a gatekeeping mechanism (only needs are fulfilled). Make it a cognitive exercise. Wants can still be fulfilled — the point is that the child knows what category they are in.

### 7. Space Ownership

**Developing well:** The child leaves spaces in acceptable condition without being told. Their room may not be perfect, but it is navigable. Shared spaces are not degraded after their use.

**Developing slowly:** The child habitually leaves messes and does not connect their behavior to the state of the space. Cleanup requires explicit instruction every time.

### 8. Weekly Review Engagement

**Developing well:** The child participates in the weekly review honestly. They name things that went well and things that were hard. They are not performing — they are reflecting.

**Developing slowly:** The child gives rote answers ("fine," "I don't know," "nothing was hard") or becomes defensive when asked about struggles.

**How to improve engagement:** Make it conversational, not interrogative. Share your own week first: "This week I struggled with ___. What about you?" Model the honesty you are asking for. Keep it short — 5-10 minutes maximum for young children.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Confusing Compliance with Formation
A child who obeys because they are afraid of your anger is compliant. A child who obeys because they have internalized the value of the routine is being formed. The first produces a person who behaves well only under observation. The second produces a person who behaves well because it is who they are. If your child's compliance disappears the moment you leave the room, the formation is not happening — only the compliance.

### Inconsistency as the Primary Failure Mode
The single most common IC1.1 failure is adult inconsistency. The standard is stated but not maintained. The consequence is threatened but not followed through. The routine is established on Monday and abandoned by Friday. The child learns: stated standards are not real standards. This lesson transfers to everything.

### Over-Correcting
Constant correction produces a child who needs constant correction. If you are correcting more than a few times per day on the same issues, something structural is wrong: the expectation is unclear, the routine is too complex, or the consequence is not connected to the behavior. Step back and diagnose the system before adding more corrections.

### Praise-Dependency
Excessive praise for routine compliance ("Great job brushing your teeth!") creates a child who expects external validation for baseline behavior. The goal is that routine compliance is unremarkable — it is simply what you do. Save genuine praise for genuine achievement: the first time the child completes the full routine without any prompting, or the time they cleaned up something they did not mess up.

### Comparing to Other Children
Every child develops self-regulation on their own timeline. A 5-year-old who cannot sit still for 10 minutes is not deficient — they are 5. A 9-year-old who cannot sit still for 10 minutes may have a maturity gap. Compare the child to their own trajectory, not to other children.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Morning Routine
**Strong:** Detailed, sequential, shows ownership. "I wake up, go to the bathroom, brush my teeth, get dressed, make my bed, then go eat breakfast." Can identify what they do best and what they sometimes skip.
**Weak:** "I get ready." No detail, no self-awareness about gaps.

### Prompt 2: Chore Ownership
**Strong:** Knows their chore, describes the standard, can describe how it feels to finish it. "I feed the dog every morning. I give him one cup of food and fill his water bowl. I feel good because he depends on me."
**Weak:** "I sometimes do stuff." No specific ownership.

### Prompt 3: Waiting
**Strong:** Can recall a specific instance. Describes what they did while waiting. Acknowledges that it was hard but does not dramatize.
**Weak:** Cannot recall an example, or every example ends with "and then I got it anyway" (suggesting the wait was negotiated away).

### Prompt 4: Want vs. Need
**Strong:** Gives concrete examples. "I need food when I'm hungry. I want candy, but I don't need it." The categorization is accurate.
**Weak:** Everything is a need, or they cannot distinguish between the categories.

### Prompt 5: Character Story
**Strong:** Names a specific person and a specific choice. Can articulate what made it hard and why they did it anyway. Shows engagement with the story, not just recitation.
**Weak:** "A person was brave." No specifics.

### Prompt 6: Hardest Part of the Week
**Strong:** Honest. Names a specific struggle — waiting, doing a chore they disliked, stopping an activity they wanted to continue. This is the most revealing prompt because it requires self-awareness about difficulty.
**Weak:** "Nothing was hard" (either dishonest or not yet self-aware enough to identify the struggle).
